task.title: Reject already-stale expected versions before payload encoding
task.objective: Complete issue https://github.com/Knosence9/project-vela/issues/398 by returning the stable optimistic-concurrency error before serializing an event when the supplied expectation is already stale, without weakening the atomic append boundary.
acceptance_criteria: An already-stale expectation returns EventLogError::WrongExpectedVersion before Serialize::serialize is invoked.
The rejected append writes no event and preserves the existing stable error context.
A matching preflight does not hold SQLite's immediate writer lock during caller-defined serialization.
The log repeats expected-version matching inside the immediate transaction so a concurrent writer cannot cause a stale append to commit.
The observable ordering is documented and the pinned local and pull-request quality gates pass.

attempts.summary: Added a focused regression using a serializer that panics if a stale append reaches payload encoding.
attempts.diagnostic: The RED test panicked with 'a stale append must be rejected before encoding', proving EventLog::append serialized the event before checking the current stream version.

attempts.summary: Moved payload encoding after expected-version matching inside the existing immediate transaction.
attempts.patch: The focused regression passed and stale expectations returned WrongExpectedVersion before serialization.

attempts.summary: Ran an independent pre-commit review of the first passing implementation.
attempts.diagnostic: The review identified a concurrency regression: caller-defined serialization could now hold SQLite's global immediate writer lock for arbitrarily long CPU or user-code execution.
attempts.patch: Replaced the lock-holding implementation with an unlocked current-version preflight, payload encoding, and a repeated authoritative expected-version check inside the immediate transaction. Shared helpers keep both checks consistent.

attempts.summary: Evaluated CodeRabbit's suggestion to move serialization back inside the immediate transaction.
attempts.diagnostic: The suggestion would reintroduce the writer-lock regression already found by independent review and was therefore not applicable to the intended single-node concurrency boundary.
attempts.patch: Kept serialization outside the transaction, documented the two-phase boundary, replied with the lock-scope rationale, and resolved the review thread without changing code.

attempts.summary: Verified the corrected implementation and required pull-request checks through squash merge.
attempts.diagnostic: The focused test and just verify passed, independent re-review found no blocking regressions, Rust quality gate run 29322743599 passed, CodeRabbit completed successfully, and pull request 399 squash-merged as a376595.
attempts.patch: No further corrective patch was required after the second review.

outcome.summary: Append now avoids serialization for already-stale expectations, retains the previous writer-lock scope for matching calls, and repeats the version check transactionally so optimistic concurrency remains atomic.

verification.command: nix develop --command cargo test --locked -p vela-kernel --test event_log rejects_stale_expected_versions_before_encoding -- --exact
Independent Codex pre-commit review for pull request 399: writer-lock finding corrected; re-review found no blocking regressions
GitHub Rust quality gate run 29322743599 for pull request 399
CodeRabbit review for pull request 399: required status passed; conflicting lock-scope suggestion was answered and resolved with rationale
nix develop --command cargo run --locked -p vela-dev -- record validate corpus/development/0019-stale-append-preflight.json # result: valid development record
nix develop --command cargo run --locked -p vela-dev -- corpus inspect corpus/development # result: 19 valid, 0 invalid

lessons: A deterministic concurrency mismatch should preempt expensive or fallible payload encoding when the stale state is already observable.
Do not hold a database writer lock across caller-defined serialization merely to improve error precedence; use an unlocked preflight and preserve an authoritative transactional recheck.
A preflight is an optimization and diagnostic boundary, not the concurrency authority: only the repeated check inside the immediate transaction protects insertion from races.
Independent review can expose a regression in an otherwise GREEN fix; record the rejected design and the correction rather than preserving only the final patch.
Automated review suggestions should be verified against the repository's concurrency guarantees and declined with explicit rationale when they reintroduce a known defect.
